serving.natural_language_portion: A typical shared serving of chilli sauce with vegetarian prawn at a Zi Char restaurant is approximately 200 g, usually consumed as part of a multi-dish meal with rice.

nutrient_source.title: Chilli sauce (cooked, with mixed protein) — estimated from IMR Malaysia MyFCD chilli-based sauce entries and USDA FoodData Central analogous sauce components
nutrient_source.organization: IMR Malaysia MyFCD; USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'chilli sauce with vegetarian prawn' exists in any approved primary source. Values are estimated by combining IMR Malaysia MyFCD data for chilli-based sauces (sambal, chilli sauce) with USDA FoodData Central data for plant-based protein components and egg. The vegetarian prawn component is modelled as a soy-protein-based mock seafood product. Macros represent a weighted composite of sauce base (~60%) and vegetarian prawn component (~40%) per 100 g of the combined dish. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed vegetable and sauce dishes — analogous estimate based on chilli sauce and soy-protein components
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chilli sauce with vegetarian prawn. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous low-GI sauce dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database: mixed vegetable sauces and soy-protein dishes consistently fall in the low GI range (GI 30–45). The dish is predominantly sauce-based with low net carbohydrate content, supporting a low GI classification. GL per serving = (35 × 7.4 × 200/100) / 100 = 5.18, rounded to 5.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g; ~1040 mg per 200 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content, which can contribute significantly to daily sodium intake; a 200 g serving provides approximately 45% of the 2300 mg daily sodium limit recommended by health authorities.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and moderate carbohydrate content (~7.4 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish has a low glycemic index and moderate carbohydrate load, making it generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions as part of a balanced meal; however, the sauce may contain added sugar which should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat content with cholesterol from egg component
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~520 mg/100 g) poses a cardiovascular risk for individuals with heart disease; the saturated fat content is moderate (1.1 g/100 g) and the dish contains cholesterol (~45 mg/100 g) from egg, so portion control and frequency of consumption should be managed carefully.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chilli Sauce with Vegetarian Prawn is a Malaysian-Singaporean Zi Char-style condiment dish consisting of a rich, spicy chilli-based sauce served with plant-based mock prawn (vegetarian prawn made from soy protein or konjac). The sauce is typically prepared by stir-frying fresh chillies, garlic, ginger, and tomato in oil, then incorporating egg to create a thick, glossy, moderately spicy sauce. The vegetarian prawn component is a processed soy- or konjac-based product designed to mimic the texture of real prawn. The dish is served as a shared side at Zi Char restaurants and is commonly eaten with steamed white rice. Nutritionally, it provides moderate protein (~5.2 g/100 g) from the soy-based mock prawn and egg, moderate fat (~5.8 g/100 g), and relatively low carbohydrate (~7.4 g/100 g), giving it a low glycemic index of approximately 35. The primary nutritional concern is its high sodium content (~520 mg/100 g), which is clinically relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is suitable for vegetarians (contains egg) but not vegans. It is generally halal-friendly when prepared without pork-derived ingredients. The chilli component contributes capsaicin, which has been associated with anti-inflammatory properties, and the dish provides meaningful amounts of vitamin C (~12 mg/100 g) and vitamin A (~38 mcg RAE/100 g) from the chilli and tomato base.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for 'chilli sauce with vegetarian prawn' exists in any approved primary source (IMR Malaysia MyFCD, HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central). Nutrient values are estimated from analogous chilli sauce and soy-protein component data from IMR Malaysia MyFCD and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated composites; no exact approved-source entry exists for this specific dish. IMR Malaysia MyFCD chilli sauce entries and USDA FoodData Central soy-protein and egg data were used as the estimation basis. 2. The food name in the input ('Vegetrain Anprawn') appears to be a menu-specific trade name; 'Vegetarian Prawn' is used as the canonical English name. 'Anprawn' is retained as an alias. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this estimated composite in the source databases. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this composite. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero as the dish uses vegetable oil and plant-based ingredients with no industrial trans-fat sources. 5. GI set to 35 (low) based on analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for mixed vegetable sauce and soy-protein dishes. GL per 200 g serving = (35 × 7.4 × 200/100) / 100 = 5.18, rounded to 5.2. 6. The dish is classified as vegetarian (contains egg) but not vegan. Halal status assumed based on plant-based and egg ingredients; actual halal certification depends on restaurant sourcing. 7. Cuisine is Malaysian with strong Singaporean Zi Char context; multilingual names provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish requirements.
